howItWorks.intro: This three phase power calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Apparent Power (kVA) = 1.732 x Line Voltage x Line Current / 1000
Real Power (kW) = kVA x Power Factor
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What is power factor?
faq.answer: Power factor is the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). A PF of 1.0 means all power does useful work. Motors and inductive loads lower PF to 0.7-0.9. Low PF wastes energy and increases utility bills.

faq.question: What is the difference between line and phase voltage?
faq.answer: Line voltage is measured between any two phases (e.g., 480V). Phase voltage is measured from one phase to neutral (480/1.732 = 277V). Lighting often uses phase voltage; motors use line voltage.

faq.question: Is the Three Phase Power Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What line voltage should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate line voltage value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Three Phase Power Calculator?
sections.body: The Three Phase Power Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your line voltage, line current, power factor, you get instant results including real power, apparent power, reactive power. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting real power right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your line voltage and need to find the right real power. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Line Voltage (V)

howToSteps.description: Type or select your line current in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Power Factor
howToSteps.description: Type or select your power factor in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your real power, apparent power, reactive power. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your line voltage before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both real power and apparent power — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your power factor, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
